a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts input also covers the full six-digit industry and could be materially different for capital-intensive dredging and marine contractors.
a: Anthropic usage reflects one model ecosystem and digital tasks, not realized substitution or avoidable hiring.
a: No source isolates already-automated tasks, so that exclusion is judgmental.
rho: Only 10% of RICS responses came from the Americas, and the survey covers construction broadly.
rho: Adoption sentiment does not prove durable labor savings.
rho: The selected firms may have lower digital maturity than large contractors but higher incentive to automate scarce technical staff.
e: No source reports the share of 237990 LMM firms in the narrowed cluster; the range is a bounded classification judgment.
e: The frozen firm-count input covers every 237990 business model, so eligibility carries unusually wide uncertainty.
e: NAICS is assigned at establishment level and may not describe a consolidated firm's dominant revenue.
s5: The succession survey spans engineering and construction rather than the selected specialty.
s5: Only 44% of the survey sample had revenue below $20 million, so it is larger than much of the target population.
s5: A full ownership and leadership transition can take eight to twelve years according to the source.
s5: Marine equipment, bonding, environmental history, and concentrated agency relationships can either improve strategic value or prevent transfer.
q: Federal practices do not cover every state, local, port, or private customer.
q: The district forecast is one geography and is informational and subject to change.
q: The estimate concerns repricing and benefit sharing only; implementation failure and demand changes are excluded.
d5: Census categories do not map one-to-one to the narrowed NAICS lens and the cited trend is nominal.
d5: Backlogs represent need, not funded demand, and can persist without contractor revenue.
d5: Federal civil-works funding is politically and annually appropriated; individual large projects create lumpy results.
d5: Storm events can accelerate emergency work but also delay planned construction.
o: No source directly measures the five-year operator-required share.
o: The estimate applies to the narrowed physical service basket, not engineering-only, monitoring-only, or excluded 237990 activities.
o: Prime accountability can remain even if subcontractors or autonomous machines perform more production.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Other Heavy and Civil Engineering Construction

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project, option-year, and task-order revenue from USACE, port and navigation authorities, federal and state agencies, municipalities, dam and levee owners, marine-terminal owners, and larger primes; work is commonly competitively procured and paid on firm-fixed, lump-sum, unit-price, or mixed terms, with recurring demand from sedimentation, inspection-driven repairs, rehabilitation, flood protection, and navigation maintenance.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code is materially heterogeneous: the official definition combines water-resource and marine work with railroads, subways, parks, golf courses, tunnels, horizontal drilling, hydroelectric facilities, and nuclear-waste-site construction. I retained the explicitly named water-resource and marine cluster because it shares specialized marine/heavy equipment, public or infrastructure-owner customers, environmental and navigation constraints, and recurring maintenance demand; the narrowing is for analytical coherence, not attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Railroad and subway construction, parks and trails, golf-course construction, tunnels, horizontal drilling, hydroelectric generating stations, nuclear-waste disposal sites, standalone earth-retention work outside water or marine projects, captive units, shells, joint-venture vehicles without standalone operations, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market contractors repeatedly serving external customers in the coherent water-resource and marine heavy-civil cluster: dredging and channel work, land drainage, flood-control structures, dams and levees, docks, marine facilities, marine pile work, and associated reconstruction, rehabilitation, maintenance, and repair.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and wages for all NAICS 237900 firms, not the narrowed water-resource and marine 237990 lens, while Anthropic measures task-level LLM exposure and actual use across occupations rather than wage-weighted substitution in these contractors. I map high exposure to estimating, project controls, accounting, administration, reporting, compliance, document review, and some engineering/management tasks; partial exposure to supervisors; and little exposure to laborers, equipment operators, divers, vessel crews, welders, drivers, and mechanics. The broad-code occupation mix may misstate the narrowed cluster in either direction, while current LLM use likely understates multimodal monitoring potential and overstates labor removal where verification is mandatory.
caveats: The OEWS table is four-digit NAICS 237900 and covers all business models in the full code, not the narrowed lens.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input also covers the full six-digit industry and could be materially different for capital-intensive dredging and marine contractors.
Anthropic usage reflects one model ecosystem and digital tasks, not realized substitution or avoidable hiring.
No source isolates already-automated tasks, so that exclusion is judgmental.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task diaries and pre/post deployment time studies from 20-30 dredging, marine, dam, levee, and flood-control contractors, split among estimating, hydrographic data processing, project controls, compliance, finance, field supervision, vessel crews, equipment operators, divers, and craft labor.
a.rationale: The measured workforce is still predominantly physical: construction and extraction occupations are 58.78% of employment, including laborers at 25.11%, equipment operators at 13.87%, and first-line construction supervisors at 8.63%; transportation and material moving adds 6.28%. The higher-wage exposed pool includes management at 8.79%, business and financial operations at 5.18%, office support at 6.40%, and architecture and engineering at 2.95%. That supports a material but minority wage-weighted opportunity concentrated above the field-production layer.

d5.bridge: Census measures nominal conservation-and-development construction, a category containing dams and levees but not perfectly matching all marine and dredging work, while ASCE compiles condition and funding needs across infrastructure categories rather than forecasting constant-price contractor quantity. I combine recent spending momentum with aging assets and funded maintenance needs, then widen materially for inflation, appropriations, project timing, and the code-to-category mismatch. Recent nominal growth overstates real quantity, while the published backlog understates demand that remains unfunded.
caveats: Census categories do not map one-to-one to the narrowed NAICS lens and the cited trend is nominal.
Backlogs represent need, not funded demand, and can persist without contractor revenue.
Federal civil-works funding is politically and annually appropriated; individual large projects create lumpy results.
Storm events can accelerate emergency work but also delay planned construction.
d5.change_evidence: Enacted annual USACE Civil Works work plans, Harbor Maintenance Trust Fund outlays, WRDA 2026 authorizations and appropriations, port capital plans, state dam and levee grants, SAM.gov award volumes, and Census construction spending deflated with appropriate heavy-civil input-price indices through 2031.
d5.rationale: Public conservation-and-development construction was $5.535 billion through May 2026 versus $4.785 billion a year earlier, a 15.7% nominal increase; full-year 2025 conservation and development was 8.5% above 2024. Structural needs are substantial: the water-resources project backlog at USACE is reported at more than $100 billion, the nonfederal dam rehabilitation need at $165 billion, and the Harbor Maintenance Trust Fund annual figure at $3.5 billion. These support modest real growth in the base, but appropriations and project slippage justify a wide downside and upside range.

caveats: No source reports the share of 237990 LMM firms in the narrowed cluster; the range is a bounded classification judgment.
The frozen firm-count input covers every 237990 business model, so eligibility carries unusually wide uncertainty.
NAICS is assigned at establishment level and may not describe a consolidated firm's dominant revenue.
e.change_evidence: A stratified classification of 200 current 237990 firms using SAM.gov, Census/CBP business registers, state contractor licenses, websites, and project histories, coding primary specialty, customer mix, repeat awards, standalone assets and workforce, operating status, captive status, and transferability.
e.rationale: The official code explicitly contains water-resource projects, dredging, drainage, marine facilities, channels, dams, docks, and flood-control work, but also many excluded production models. Within the retained cluster, most active standalone contractors sell outsourced project services repeatedly to agencies, infrastructure owners, or primes. The range reflects uncertainty about the cluster's share of LMM firms and removes captive operations, non-operating entities, one-project vehicles, businesses without repeat external work, and firms lacking transferable crews, equipment, permits, bonding, or customer relationships.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 237990 ($13,351,101,919) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 237990 ($37,005,250,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0796 (margins.json[23]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Engineering/Construction EBITDA/Sales 7.96%); revenue/employee $448,804 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 237990); class <5: 2,391 firms, avg revenue/firm $783,434 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $62,361 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 849 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,054,528 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $163,540 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 559 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,129,288 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $328,691 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 565 firms, avg revenue/firm $14,535,508 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,157,026 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 110 firms, avg revenue/firm $76,626,882 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,099,500 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 59 firms, avg revenue/firm $244,711,441 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $19,479,031 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 675

caveats: No source directly measures the five-year operator-required share.
The estimate applies to the narrowed physical service basket, not engineering-only, monitoring-only, or excluded 237990 activities.
Prime accountability can remain even if subcontractors or autonomous machines perform more production.
o.change_evidence: USACE and port data on government-fleet versus contracted dredging, autonomous dredge and equipment deployments, crew-hours per constant cubic yard or project unit, prime-contractor clauses, safety and environmental enforcement, and owner self-performance decisions through 2031.
o.rationale: Dredging, dam and levee work, channels, docks, and marine construction require physical mobilization, vessels and heavy equipment, site control, environmental compliance, surveying, safe execution, and contractual accountability. AI can optimize plans, quantities, schedules, monitoring, reporting, and equipment control but cannot replace the responsible operating contractor within five years. The downside permits some owner self-performance, autonomous equipment, remote surveying, and elimination of selected planning or monitoring tasks while retaining an accountable operator for the service as a whole.

q.bridge: The FAR specifies federal construction pricing methods and the USACE forecast shows concrete 237990 projects and set-asides, but neither measures discounted retention of AI savings. I infer initial retention when productivity improves delivery under a firm-fixed, lump-sum, or unit-price award, then reduce it for sealed bidding, government estimates, transparent quantities, future competition, negotiated modifications, and option pricing. Specialized equipment, mobilization, local disposal constraints, and a limited qualified bidder pool can make the broad federal-pricing evidence understate retention; repeated low-price procurement can make it overstate retention.
caveats: Federal practices do not cover every state, local, port, or private customer.
The district forecast is one geography and is informational and subject to change.
The estimate concerns repricing and benefit sharing only; implementation failure and demand changes are excluded.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level bid tabs, government estimates, option-year prices, bidder counts, gross margins, change orders, and cost-to-complete data before and after AI deployment, segmented by dredging method, disposal constraint, mobilization distance, contract type, set-aside, and repeat customer.
q.rationale: Federal construction generally uses firm-fixed-price contracts on lump-sum, unit-price, or mixed terms, allowing contractors to retain in-contract administrative and project-control savings if scope and quantities do not change. Retention erodes at rebid as agencies and competitors learn lower costs. The San Francisco District forecast shows numerous separately competed 237990 dredging and dam projects, including small-business set-asides, confirming both repeat external demand and recurring price discovery; specialized marine assets and execution risk prevent complete pass-through.

rho.bridge: RICS surveyed more than 2,200 global construction professionals about organizational AI adoption and barriers, not US LMM water-resource and marine contractors or the share of exposed labor implemented in five years. I translate the current adoption distribution, stated use cases, and barriers into a five-year implementation fraction, reducing it for fragmented project data, remote and marine operations, compliance, safety, bonding, and human review. The professional-heavy sample may overstate small-contractor readiness, while embedded construction software may make current self-reported adoption understate future diffusion.
caveats: Only 10% of RICS responses came from the Americas, and the survey covers construction broadly.
Adoption sentiment does not prove durable labor savings.
The selected firms may have lower digital maturity than large contractors but higher incentive to automate scarce technical staff.
rho.change_evidence: A US survey of 237990 water/marine contractors by revenue and specialty recording production deployment, approved budgets, workflow-level labor hours, bid accuracy, survey-data processing, rework, compliance errors, abandoned pilots, and realized headcount or hiring changes over at least 12 months.
rho.rationale: Current construction deployment is shallow: 45% reported no implementation, 34% early pilots, just under 12% regular use in specific processes, 1.5% use across multiple processes, and less than 1% organization-wide embedding. Estimating, scheduling, progress tracking, contract review, regulatory documentation, hydrographic-data interpretation, and project reporting offer implementable use cases, but errors can create costly quantity, environmental, safety, and navigational consequences. The base therefore implements well under half of exposed opportunity.

s5.bridge: FMI/CFMA measures stated exits and ownership planning among almost 300 US engineering-and-construction executives, not completed qualifying control transfers in LMM water-resource and marine contractors. I start below the reported 38% planning to exit in three to five years, then discount for unexecuted plans, internal continuity without control change, liquidation, long transition periods, and businesses made less transferable by bonding, specialized equipment, key operators, permits, and claims. The executive sample may skew larger and more transition-aware than the lens, while unplanned sales bias the source downward.
caveats: The succession survey spans engineering and construction rather than the selected specialty.
Only 44% of the survey sample had revenue below $20 million, so it is larger than much of the target population.
A full ownership and leadership transition can take eight to twelve years according to the source.
Marine equipment, bonding, environmental history, and concentrated agency relationships can either improve strategic value or prevent transfer.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, BizBuySell, SBA loan, ESOP, SAM.gov novation, contractor-license, and vessel/equipment title records matched to an eligible 237990 denominator for 2021-2026, separating third-party control sales, genuine family/management control changes, ESOPs, liquidations, and non-control recapitalizations.
s5.rationale: FMI/CFMA reports that 38% of owners plan to exit in three to five years and 51% of that group lacks a defined transition plan. Across respondents, 64% intend an internal transfer, 8% an external sale, 25% are uncertain, and the report says only a small share of E&C firms are saleable to a third party. These data indicate real succession pressure but a qualifying five-year control-transfer probability materially below stated exit intentions.

principal_driver: Recurring navigation maintenance and aging dam, levee, flood-control, and marine assets sustain operator-required physical demand while AI improves scarce estimating and project-control labor.
principal_weakness: The official code and frozen inputs span radically different businesses, while the selected market remains exposed to lumpy public funding and repeated competitive price discovery.
